coredata.dc:title: A facile fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots and Fe3O4 nanoplates for the detection of hyaluronidase activity
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Qiutong
Rong, Sihan
Cen, Yao
Xu, Guanhong
Xie, Zhen
Yang, Jing
Sun, Yong
Hu, Qin
Wei, Fangdi
coredata.dc:description: Since the sensitivity of the sensing platform using carbon dots (CDs) alone is poor, the development of sensors based on the combination of CDs with other nanomaterials has attracted considerable interest. In this paper, a novel and facile method was established using CDs and Fe3O4 nanoplates (NPs) for the determination of hyaluronidase (HAase) activity. Fe3O4 NPs were introduced to quench the fluorescence of CDs through inner filter effect (IFE) for the first time. The negatively charged HA could bind to the positively charged Fe3O4 NPs via electrostatic attraction to form HA/Fe3O4 NPs complex, resulting in the weakening of the IFE between Fe3O4 NPs and CDs. Hence, the fluorescence of CDs was recovered. With the presence of HAase, HA was hydrolyzed into small fragments which were unable to interact with Fe3O4 NPs. Thus, the fluorescence of CDs was “turn-off” again by Fe3O4 NPs. The activity of HAase was determined in a range of 0.0040−0.12 U mL−1 with a limit of detection of 0.0016 U mL−1. Both the CDs and Fe3O4 NPs were label-free, low-cost and eco-friendly, making the detection system simple and economical. The proposed method provides a new way of designing new sensing platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3O4 nanoplates
Inner filter effect
Hyaluronic acid
Hyaluronidase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of HAase activity in buffer solutions
Detection of HAase activity in urine samples
Synthesis and characterization of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs
Anti-interference and selectivity of the system for HAase detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. (A) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (B) The TEM image of CDs; insert: HRTEM image. (C) The XPS spectrum of CDs. (D) The FT-IR spectrum of CDs.

Characterization of Fe3O4 NPs. (A) The SEM image of Fe3O4 NPs; insert: an individual Fe3O4 NPs image. (B) The AFM image of Fe3O4 NPs; insert: the corresponding height profile along the line. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe3O4 NPs. (D) The FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4 NPs.

Principle of HAase detection. (A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs towards different concentrations of Fe3O4 NPs. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe3O4 NPs and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (C) The zeta potential histogram of Fe3O4 NPs, CDs, HA and HA/Fe3O4 NPs complex. (D) The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs before and after adding Fe3O4 NPs. (E) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs + HA, CDs + HAase and CDs + HA + HAase. (F) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs + Fe3O4 NPs, CDs + Fe3O4 NPs + HA, CDs + Fe3O4 NPs + HAase and CDs + Fe3O4 NPs + HA + HAase.
Optimization of experimental conditions. (A) reaction time, (B) pH, (C) concentrations of Fe3O4 NPs and (D) concentrations of HA.

Quantitative determination of HAase. (A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the HA/Fe3O4 NPs/CDs system towards various concentrations of HAase. (B) The curve of the fluorescence intensity of CDs versus the concentration of HAase in the range of 0-0.60 U·mL−1. Insert: calibration curve of HAase detection in the range of 0.0040-0.12 U·mL−1.

Anti-interference and selectivity of the system for HAase detection. (A) Anti-interference toward ions and small molecules. (B) Selectivity of HAase detection toward enzymes. The concentration of HAase was 0.20 U·mL−1, Na+ and K+ were 100 mM, Ca2+, Mg2+, NH4 + were 10 mM, small molecules like Crea, Urea and Uric Acid (UA) were 1.0 mM, Glutathione (Glu), Cysteine (Cys) and Ascorbic Acid (AA) were 0.1 mM, and various enzymes such as glucose oxidase (GOX), Cholesterol Oxidase (COD), β-Galactosidase (β-Gal), Alkaline Phosphatase (ALP), Butyrylcholinesterase (BChE), β-Glucosidase (β-Glu), α-Glucosidase (α-Glu), Pyrophosphatase (PPase), and trypsin (Try) were all 0.50 U⋅mL−1.
Schematic illustration of CDs and Fe3O4 NPs based fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of HAase activity.

head.ce:title: A facile fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots and Fe3O4 nanoplates for the detection of hyaluronidase activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cardiovascular & Cerebrovascular Medicine, School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Cardiovascular & Cerebrovascular Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Cardiovascular & Cerebrovascular Medicine, School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Toxicology, Ningde Normal University, Ningde, Fujian, 352000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key laboratory of toxicology, Ningde normal university, Ningde, Fujian 352000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since the sensitivity of the sensing platform using carbon dots (CDs) alone is poor, the development of sensors based on the combination of CDs with other nanomaterials has attracted considerable interest. In this paper, a novel and facile method was established using CDs and Fe3O4 nanoplates (NPs) for the determination of hyaluronidase (HAase) activity. Fe3O4 NPs were introduced to quench the fluorescence of CDs through inner filter effect (IFE) for the first time. The negatively charged HA could bind to the positively charged Fe3O4 NPs via electrostatic attraction to form HA/Fe3O4 NPs complex, resulting in the weakening of the IFE between Fe3O4 NPs and CDs. Hence, the fluorescence of CDs was recovered. With the presence of HAase, HA was hydrolyzed into small fragments which were unable to interact with Fe3O4 NPs. Thus, the fluorescence of CDs was “turn-off” again by Fe3O4 NPs. The activity of HAase was determined in a range of 0.0040−0.12 U mL−1 with a limit of detection of 0.0016 U mL−1. Both the CDs and Fe3O4 NPs were label-free, low-cost and eco-friendly, making the detection system simple and economical. The proposed method provides a new way of designing new sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as a new class of carbon nanomaterials have aroused considerable attention. They have excellent optical properties like high quantum yield, tunable fluorescence emission and superior photo-stability [1]. Compared to traditional quantum dots (QDs) using heavy metals as raw materials, CDs have characters of lower cytotoxicity, better biocompatibility, lower cost and greater environmental friendliness [2]. The employment of CDs in sensing, drug delivery, bioimaging has been widely developed [3,4]. Our group has synthesized CDs with outstanding optical properties using 1, 2, 4-triaminobenzene (TAB) and ethylene imine polymer (PEI) as raw materials through a one-pot hydrothermal process. The CDs have been successfully used to deliver small interfering RNA into human glioblastoma cells for cellular imaging and anticancer therapy [5]. However, the potential applications of the CDs in the field of analytical chemistry have not been explored.

Hyaluronidase (HAase), as a subclass of endoglycosidase, can specifically hydrolyze 1,4-linkages between N-acetyl-β-D-glucosamine and D-glucuronate residues in hyaluronic acid (HA) [6]. HAase is concerned with a variety of physiological and pathological processes including embryogenesis, fertilization, inflammation, and wound healing [7–10]. The overexpression of HAase is closely associated with the growth of many malignant tumor cells such as bladder [11], prostate [12], breast [13] and colorectal [14] cancer at the early stages. Therefore, HAase is considered as a potential tumor marker [15] and the evaluation of HAase activity is of significance for the diagnosis and therapy of cancer. During the past decades, several physicochemical methods have been exploited for the detection of HAase activity, involving turbidimetry [16], viscosimetry [17], colorimetry [18] and zymography [19]. But they either lack sensitivity and selectivity or need expensive antibodies and reagents. Fluorescent assay has drawn more and more concern on account of its high sensitivity and simple operation. There have been reported several fluorescent sensing platforms for HAase activity assay [20–29]. Among these diverse fluorescent probes (e. g noble metal nanoparticles, Organic fluorophores, QDs and CDs), CDs are outstanding because of their superb properties. Nevertheless, the sensing platform using CDs alone is not sensitive enough [22]. Although some efforts have been made to combine CDs with other nanomaterials to improve the analytical sensitivity, the process of combination needs extra modifications, bringing laborious procedures and increasing cost [21,27]. Therefore, searching for suitable nanomaterials to combine with CDs simply and use them to detect analyte sensitively is highly demanded.

To date, a number of magnetite (Fe3O4) nanomaterials with various morphologies like nanorods [30], nanotubes [31], nanoplates [32], and nanoflowers [33] have been intensively investigated. It has been validated that the properties of materials can be affected by morphology, the direction of crystal growth and the exposed facets [34]. In particular, Fe3O4 nanoplates (NPs) with anisotropic morphologies are expected to show extraordinary physical and chemical properties [35]. Fe3O4 NPs exhibit superior performances in energy storage, catalysts, water treatment and magnetic resonance imaging because of their special electrical properties, magnetic properties, biocompatibility, environmental benignity, etc [36–39]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no literature has been reported about the application of Fe3O4 NPs in fluorometric analysis. We found that Fe3O4 NPs could be exploited as an excellent fluorescence quencher due to their large surface areas and wide UV–vis absorption [40,41].

Herein, we synthesized N-doped CDs and Fe3O4 NPs through a simple solvothermal process and established a facile fluorescent sensing platform for the sensitive detection of HAase activity. As outlined in Scheme 1 , the CDs with strong fluorescence could be quenched by Fe3O4 NPs. The presence of HA weakened the interaction between Fe3O4 NPs and CDs by forming HA/Fe3O4 NPs complex so that the fluorescence of CDs was preserved. HAase specifically cleaved HA into small fragments through enzymatic hydrolysis which made HA fail to bind to Fe3O4 NPs. As a result, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched again. The fluorescence of CDs had a direct relationship with HAase activity. The detection process was simple to implement and the proposed strategy was confirmed to have the lowest detection limit compared with other CDs-based detection platforms.

HAase from bovine testes was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (St. Louis, Mo, U.S.A.). Hyaluronic acid sodium salt from Streptococcus equi, 1,2,4-Triaminobenzene dihydrochloride (TAB), ethylene imine polymer (PEI, M.W.1800), ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ethylene glycol (EG) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were analytical grade reagents. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (Milli-Q, Millipore). The characterization details are provided in the Supporting Information.

The CDs were synthesized according to our previous study [5]. In brief, TAB (0.1 g) and PEI (0.4 g) were mixed into 7 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated until dissolved completely. Then the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After reacted at 130℃ for 3 h, the products were cooled down to room temperature naturally and dialyzed for 6 h to remove unreacted materials. Finally, the as-prepared CDs were precipitated by ethanol and petroleum ether. The precipitates were washed several times and dried under vacuum at 60℃.

Fe3O4 NPs were prepared by aging Fe(OH)2 under anaerobic conditions, which was known as Schikorr reaction. The synthetic procedure was carried out as follow [42]: NaOH (0.5 g) was dissolved in 54 mL of water/EG mixtures with a volume ratio of 1:3 to obtain a homogeneous solution A. Meanwhile, FeSO4·7H2O (0.04 g) was dissolved in 6 mL of H2SO4 (0.01 M) to obtain solution B. The two solutions were bubbled with nitrogen while stirring for 2 h. Next, solution B was added to solution A at a constant rate under stirring to gain Fe(OH)2 precursor solution. After the reaction was completed, the solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and heated to 90℃ for 24 h. The obtained products were cooled down to room temperature and washed several times with ultrapure water. Finally, the as-prepared Fe3O4 NPs were dried under vacuum and kept at 4℃ for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of HAase activity in buffer solutions
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μL of different concentrations of HAase were mixed with 5 μL of HA solutions (0.3 mg mL−1) in Tris-HCl (20 mM, pH 7.4) and incubated at 37℃ for 60 min. Afterwards, Fe3O4 NPs and CDs were added separately and vortexed. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were measured with the excitation wavelength of 450 nm.

To evaluate the anti-interference and selectivity of the method, a range of potential interfering substances, including ions, small molecules and enzymes, were added into the system in the absence or presence of HAase following the same procedures above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of HAase activity in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers. They were firstly mixed with Chitosan to agglomerate the negatively charged moieties, and centrifuged for 10 min at a speed of 12,000 rpm. Then, the supernatant was collected and diluted 300 times with ultrapure water. Finally, 50 μL of the processed urine samples were used to measure the HAase activity according to section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method using TAB and PEI as raw materials. The as-prepared CDs had a broad excitation spectrum from 380 nm to 450 nm and a narrow fluorescence emission peak at 580 nm (Fig. 1 A). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image (Fig. 1B) manifested that the CDs were spherical in shape and well dispersed with an average size of 6.5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image provided further insight into the nanostructure of the CDs. It showed an obvious lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 1B), which agreed with the (100) facet of graphite and indicated that the CDs contained graphite-like structures [43]. To investigate the chemical composition of CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed. As seen in Fig. 1C, there were three typical peaks at ∼285 (C1s), ∼399 (N1s) and ∼531 eV (O1s), which confirmed the components of CDs. The contents of C, N and O were 74.31 %, 15.43 % and 10.26 %, respectively. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. 1D) demonstrated that the CDs exhibited apparent absorption bands at 3404 cm−1 (NH stretching vibration), 2947 and 2837 cm−1 (CH stretching vibration), 1628 cm−1 (CC stretching vibration), 1462 cm−1 (CH bending vibration). All these results ascertained the successful synthesis of CDs.

Fe3O4 NPs were also prepared through a facile one-pot solvothermal procedure. It can be seen from the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image in Fig. 2 A that the products had a plate-like morphology with a side length of 200−500 nm. To evaluate the thickness of the nanoplates, atomic force microscopy (AFM) was performed (Fig. 2B). The corresponding line profile indicated that the measured height was under 8 nm. The wide UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 2C revealed the potential fluorescence quenching property of Fe3O4 NPs. The FT-IR spectrum of the product (Fig. 2D) displayed one absorption peak at around 585 cm−1; this peak was attributed to the vibrations of Fe-O functional group of Fe3O4.

The CDs were served as the fluorescence signal probe, and Fe3O4 NPs were employed as a highly efficient fluorescence quencher. With the increase of Fe3O4 NPs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased gradually (Fig. 3 A). The possible quenching mechanism was explored. As can be seen from Fig. 3B, Fe3O4 NPs had a wide absorption spectrum from 300 to 700 nm, which overlapped significantly with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. It suggested that the quenching mechanism was probably caused by the IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [44]. The zeta potential of CDs and Fe3O4 NPs shown in Fig. 3C were +17.0 mV and +15.1 mV, respectively. Because both of them were positive charges, there was no possibility of electrostatic attraction between them. Their distance was far, so FRET effect would not occur between them. Fig. 3D showed that the fluorescence lifetime of CDs remained almost unchanged before and after the incorporation of Fe3O4 NPs. These results strongly proved that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Fe3O4 NPs on CDs was attributed to IFE.

Fig. 3E exhibited that the CDs had a strong fluorescent signal at 580 nm with excitation of 450 nm. The fluorescence intensity and peak location of the CDs did not show obvious changes when HA or HAase or both of them were added into CDs solution, indicating that HA and HAase had no direct interaction towards CDs.

HA had a high density of negative charges (-19.5 mV), and it could be effectively absorbed on the surface of Fe3O4 NPs (+15.1 mV) through electrostatic attraction, forming the complex of HA/Fe3O4 NPs (Fig. 3C). Fig. S1 displayed the UV–vis spectra of Fe3O4 NPs before and after HA addition. When increased concentrations of HA were added, the absorbance of Fe3O4 NPs gradually decreased, which further confirmed the form of HA/Fe3O4 NPs complex. When CDs were added into the complex solution afterwards, the IFE effect between CDs and Fe3O4 NPs could not occur, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs was retained (Fig. 3F).

In order to further study, we tried CDs-1 and CDs-2 with different properties from CDs. CDs-1 had similar excitation and emission spectra from CDs, while CDs-2 showed different fluorescent characteristics (Fig. S2A). The fluorescence of the three nanomaterials could all be quenched by Fe3O4 NPs because of IFE (Fig. S2B). However, when HA was added, the degree of fluorescence recovery was much different, CDs were the biggest, followed by CDs-1, and finally CDs-2 (Fig. S2C). The results were consistent with the zeta potential of the three nanomaterials (Fig. S2D). Both CDs and CDs-1 had positive charges. However, since the zeta potential of CDs was much higher than that of CDs-1, the fluorescence recovery of CDs was about three times as much as that of CDs-1. On the other hand, CDs-2 had negative charges, and thus they could strongly be adsorbed on the surface of the positive charged Fe3O4 NPs through electrostatic attraction. Because the addition of HA had little influence on the strong interaction, the fluorescence of CDs-2 did not recover much.

HAase could specifically cleave HA into small fragments through enzymatic hydrolysis. Thus, when it was introduced into the solution containing Fe3O4 NPs, CDs and HA, the complex of HA/Fe3O4 NPs was destroyed and Fe3O4 NPs were released. The IFE effect between Fe3O4 NPs and CDs recurred, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of CDs again. Fig. S3 demonstrated that the zeta potential of HA/Fe3O4 NPs complex increased as the concentration of HAase increased, which further confirmed the disassembly of HA/Fe3O4 NPs complex.

The fluorescence intensity of CDs kept being quenched with the addition of HAase into the solution containing Fe3O4 NPs and CDs, manifesting that HAase had no effect on the IFE between Fe3O4 NPs and CDs.
The above phenomena confirmed the feasibility of the proposed system to detect the activity of HAase based on the IFE between Fe3O4 NPs and CDs.
To achieve the best analytical performance of HAase, the effects of reaction time, pH, concentrations of Fe3O4 NPs and HA were optimized.

It was worth mentioning that the interaction between CDs and Fe3O4 NPs was quite sensitive. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched as soon as Fe3O4 NPs were added (black line in Fig. 4 A). This was attributed to the IFE, which was generally considered as a kind of instant effect [45]. The reaction between HA and Fe3O4 NPs was also very fast. After HA was added into the Fe3O4 NPs solutions, the HA/Fe3O4 NPs complex could form within 2 min, resulting in the recovery and stability of the fluorescence of CDs (blue line in Fig. 4A). The strong electrostatic attraction led to rapid assembly of HA/Fe3O4 NPs complex [46]. The reaction time of the whole analytical procedure depended on the enzymolysis of HAase, which was approximately 60 min (red line Fig. 4A).

The optimum pH was pH 7.4, at which the change of fluorescence intensity of HA/Fe3O4 NPs/CDs system was the most significant before and after the addition of HAase (Fig. 4B). It was mainly caused by three reasons: First, the fluorescence of CDs increased with the increase of pH (Fig. S4A) due to the change in surface charge caused by the protonation-deprotonation of -NH2 groups on the surface of CDs [5]. Second, the change of pH could also affect the charge state of HA and Fe3O4 NPs, which further affected the zeta potential [47]. As shown in Fig. S4B, with the increase of pH, the net negative charge of HA increased and the net positive charge of Fe3O4 NPs decreased. At near neutral pH, both HA and Fe3O4 NPs possessed relatively strong net charges, thus the electrostatic interaction between them was more robust. Third, HAase used in the experiment came from bovine testes and it has been reported that the optimum activity of testicular HAase was at neutral pH [48]. Based on the above reasons, pH 7.4 was the optimum.

With the increase of Fe3O4 NPs, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched more effectively, but the recovery after adding HA was decreased because the excessive amount of Fe3O4 NPs without binding to HA could also quench the fluorescence of CDs. The difference between the fluorescence of CDs with and without HA was the biggest when the concentration of Fe3O4 NPs was 8 μg mL−1 (Fig. 4C). Therefore, 8 μg mL−1 of Fe3O4 NPs was selected in further experiments.

The fluorescence of CDs enhanced with the increasing concentration of HA, and reached the maximum when the concentration of HA was 5 μg mL−1 (Fig. 4D). At this concentration, Fe3O4 NPs combined with HA completely and the IFE was almost all restrained. Continuing to increase the concentration of HA, excess HA would be left in the solutions, leading to the decrease of sensitivity. Thus, the concentration of HA was set at 5 μg mL−1 in this study.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased gradually with the increase of HAase concentrations from 0 to 0.60 U mL−1. The fluorescence spectra of CDs in the HA/Fe3O4 NPs/CDs system towards various concentrations of HAase were displayed in Fig. 5 A. There was a good linear relationship from 0.0040 to 0.12 U mL−1(Fig. 5B). The linear relationship equation was expressed as F = −17,892c + 3445.9 (R 2 = 0.9989). The detection limit (LOD) was 0.0016 U mL−1 according to the 3σ/K rule. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of within-run and between-run were all less than 8.6 % (Table S1). These above results verified that the as-prepared fluorescent sensing system had a high sensitivity and good reproducibility with both accuracy and precision in the analysis of HAase activity.

Table S2 exhibited the comparison of the proposed assay with other previously reported fluorescent assay using CDs as fluorescent probes for the determination of HAase activity. The sensing platform we built had a lower LOD, which attributed to the high quenching efficiency of Fe3O4 NPs and the sensitive electrostatic adsorption effect between HA and Fe3O4 NPs.
In summary, we utilized CDs and Fe3O4 NPs as the probe which was eco-friendly and low-cost. The detection process was facile, and the method has been proved to be sensitive and accurate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference and selectivity of the system for HAase detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the anti-interference and selectivity of HAase assay, a series of potential interfering substances including ions, small molecules and enzymes were examined under the same conditions. Fig. 6 A showed that common ions and small molecules had no distinct interference in the HAase assay platform. Fig. 6B revealed that other enzymes could not cause the change of fluorescence signal. The HA/Fe3O4 NPs/CDs system presented a high anti-interference and selectivity towards HAase.

We further performed the measurement of HAase in human urine samples (donated from healthy volunteers) to verify the practicality of the proposed sensing system. After spiking the samples with different concentrations of HAase, the recovery of HAase in the human serum sample ranged from 99 to 104 % with RSDs below 5.1 % (Table S3). These outcomes indicated that the sensing strategy was applicable for the detection of HAase in biological samples.

We established a novel and facile fluorescent sensing platform for monitoring the activity of HAase based on the IFE between Fe3O4 NPs and CDs. Fe3O4 NPs could quench the fluorescence of CDs, whereas the fluorescence could be restored in the presence of HA which was able to bind to Fe3O4 NPs via electrostatic attraction. HAase catalyzed the hydrolysis of HA, the IFE effect between Fe3O4 NPs and CDs recurred. By measuring the changes of the fluorescence of CDs, the activity of HAase were determined. The established system proved to be sensitive and selective and could be applied in biological samples. We expanded the application scope of Fe3O4 NPs. In further study we will focus on combining the fluorescence quenching property with magnetic property of Fe3O4 NPs to gain more functions in the field of analysis.

Qiutong Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Sihan Rong: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yao Cen: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Visualization. Guanhong Xu: Formal analysis, Visualization. Zhen Xie: Investigation, Data curation. Jing Yang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Yong Sun: Formal analysis, Visualization. Qin Hu: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Fangdi Wei: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61775099, 81973283, 21705080), College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Jiangsu Province (No. 202010312086Z).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130434.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Frontiers in carbon dots: design, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, L. Wang, Y. Li, Y. Feng and W. Feng, Frontiers in carbon dots: design, properties and applications, Mater. Chem. Front. 3 (2019) 2571-2601.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Lim, W. Shen and Z. Gao, Carbon quantum dots and their applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 44 (2015) 362-381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances and perspectives in carbon dot-based fluorescent probes: mechanism, and application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, S. Zhao, B. Li, K. Yang, M. Lan and L. Zeng, Advances and perspectives in carbon dot-based fluorescent probes: mechanism, and application, Coord. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances of carbon dots in imaging-guided theranostics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.B. Chen, M.L. Liu and C.Z. Huang, Recent advances of carbon dots in imaging-guided theranostics, TrAC, Trends Anal. Chem. 134 (2021) 116116.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: PEI modified orange emissive carbon dots with excitation-independent fluorescence emission for cellular imaging and siRNA delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Li, F. Wei, X. Wu, P. Zhou, Q. Chen, Y. Cen, G. Xu, X. Cheng, A. Zhang and Q. Hu, PEI modified orange emissive carbon dots with excitation-independent fluorescence emission for cellular imaging and siRNA delivery, Carbon 177 (2021) 403-411.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure of human hyaluronidase-1, a hyaluronan hydrolyzing enzyme involved in tumor growth and angiogenesis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.L. Chao, L. Muthukumar and O. Herzberg, Structure of human hyaluronidase-1, a hyaluronan hydrolyzing enzyme involved in tumor growth and angiogenesis, Biochemistry 46 (2007) 6911-6920.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The magic glue hyaluronan and its eraser hyaluronidase: a biological overview

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.S. Girish and K. Kemparaju, The magic glue hyaluronan and its eraser hyaluronidase: a biological overview, Life Sci. 80 (2007) 1921-1943.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Expression pattern of a novel hyaluronidase during Xenopus embryogenesis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.E. Hyde and R.W. Old, Expression pattern of a novel hyaluronidase during Xenopus embryogenesis, Mech. Dev. 82 (1999) 213-217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hyaluronidase allergy: a rare cause of periorbital inflammation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Borchard, R. Puy and R. Nixon, Hyaluronidase allergy: a rare cause of periorbital inflammation, Australas. J. Dermatol. 51 (2010) 49-51.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ma, Y. Zhang, Z. Guo and N. Gu, Facile synthesis of ultrathin magnetic iron oxide nanoplates by Schikorr reaction, Nanoscale Res. Lett. 8 (2013) 16.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.L. Li, M.J. Henderson, K.Z. Wang, X.G. Tuo, Y.C. Leng, K. Xiong, Y.L. Liu, Y. Ren, J. Courtois and M.H. Yan, Colloidal assembly of magnetic nanoparticles and polyelectrolytes by arrested electrostatic interaction, Colloids Surf., A 514 (2017) 107-116.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An analysis of the dependence of the zeta potential and surface charge on surfactant concentration, ionic strength, and pH

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. J. Wnek, R. Davies, An analysis of the dependence of the zeta potential and surface charge on surfactant concentration, ionic strength, and pH, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 60 (1977) 361-375.
V.B. Lokeshwar, M.G. Selzer, Hyaluronidase, in: M. Schwab (Eds.), Encyclopedia of Cancer, Springer, Berlin, Heidelberg, 2011, pp. 1772-1775.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qiutong Chen is currently a graduate student focusing on synthesis and application of nanomaterials such as carbon dots and nanoplates at Nanjing Medical University. She was awarded a B.S. degree in pharmacy from the same university in 2019.

Sihan Rong is currently a graduate student focusing on synthesis and application of quantum dots at Nanjing Medical University. She was awarded a B.S. degree in pharmacy from the same university in 2019.

Yao Cen received her Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from Hunan University in 2016 under the mentorship of Prof. Xia Chu. She then joined the Nanjing Medical University as a lecturer. Her research interests include constructing novel functional nanostructures for bioassay, bioimaging and drug delivery.

Guanhong Xu received his M.S. degree in pharmacology from Nanjing Medical University in 2009. He joined the Nanjing Medical University as a lecturer and was promoted to associate professor in 2014. He obtained Ph.D. from Nanjing Medical University in 2021. His research interests include constructing novel functional fluorescent nanomaterials for bioassay and bioimaging.
Zhen Xie is currently an undergraduate student at Nanjing Medical University.

Jing Yang is currently an associate professor at school of pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. She obtained her M.S. on pharmaceutical analysis from China Pharmaceutical University in 2005 and obtained Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from Nanjing University in 2015. Her research interests include synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and their analytical applications in complex samples.
Yong Sun is currently a teacher at Key laboratory of toxicology, Ningde Normal University.

Qin Hu is a professor at school of pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. She obtained her Ph.D. on Analytical Chemistry from East China Normal University in 2001. She is currently conducting research in two areas: (1) synthesis of nano-silica molecular imprinted polymer and its application in analysis of environmental endocrine disrupting chemicals and (2) molecular imprinted quantum dots application in bioanalysis.

Fangdi Wei is currently an associate professor at school of pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. She obtained her M.S. on Analytical Chemistry from Nanjing University in 2002 and obtained Ph.D. from Nanjing Medical University in 2016. Her main research interests are in two areas: (1) synthesis and application of nanomaterials and (2) analysis of trace components in life system.
